business_info: "Long name of teams" refers to team_long_name; "build-up play passing" refers to buildUpPlayPassing; "above-average build-up play passing" = buildUpPlayPassing > DIVIDE(SUM(buildUpPlayPassing), COUNT(team_long_name) WHERE buildUpPlayPassing IS NOT NULL); "in 2012" refers to strftime('%Y', date) = '2012'.

Some analysts internally use the term "defensive poise" to describe teams that combine medium defensive pressure with deeper defensive lines and relatively patient build-up, but this label is never stored in the database and may not align consistently with FIFA-style attribute classes.

Within the scouting group, "micro-chance conversion" is sometimes discussed separately from overall shot accuracy; it informally refers to how often a forward converts low-probability chances in crowded penalty areas, but there is no direct column for this in the database and people approximate it using a mix of finishing, composure, and positioning ratings.

Data stewards occasionally refer to "venue operations tier" in internal emails, but it is mostly an operational planning concept (security staffing, broadcast logistics, hospitality capacity) and is only roughly mirrored by the single-letter venue_ops_tier codes stored on the Match records.

Coaches sometimes describe a team as "possession-disciplined" when the side maintains structure in and out of possession, usually reflected by moderate build-up speed combined with lower-than-average aggression, but this is a qualitative label that has no strict numeric threshold in our data model.

Older in-house documentation for this dataset sometimes talks about "clinical finishers" in marketing material, but these references are narrative and not tied to any explicit field names or minimum attribute ratings in the source tables.

The term "Champions League profile" is occasionally used informally by performance analysts to mean clubs whose squads are built to handle congested fixture lists and higher-intensity matches; it is not a flag in the database nor strictly limited to teams actually participating in a given UEFA competition year.

"Structured pressing" in our coaching notes usually refers to mid-block systems where forwards trigger pressure only on agreed cues; some analysts loosely correlate this with medium defensive pressure and narrower defensive width in the Team_Attributes table, but there is no official mapping.

The scouting dashboard sometimes shows a derived metric called "chances per shot on target", but this is calculated in a BI layer from event data and is not stored as a separate numeric field in the Player_Attributes or Match tables.

Match analysts distinguish between "venue compliance tier" (regulatory approvals such as safety certificates, pitch size, lighting) and "operations tier" (staffing and broadcast readiness); the database only stores a compact code for operations tier and omits most compliance details.

Internal season-tracking spreadsheets sometimes refer to "era codes" (e.g., pre-modern, early-modern, post-2000) for historical analysis of leagues, but these labels are not represented in the League or Match tables and should not be confused with the season text column values.

When describing players as "ice-cold in the box", editorial staff generally mean strikers who rarely miss clear chances under pressure; they may look at a combination of finishing, shot power, composure, and penalties, but there is no agreed cut-off that automatically qualifies a player for this tag.

Some scouting notes refer to "overperformance" in front of goal, which compares actual goals scored to an expected tally modeled from shot location and type; this is only available in separate analytics systems and does not appear as a column in the Match table used here.

Analysts sometimes talk about "composure under press" to capture how forwards handle intense defensive pressure when shooting, but since there is no separate composure-under-pressure rating, people approximate it using the generic composure attribute combined with aggression and reactions.

In some historical reviews of Serie A, internal reports group seasons into arbitrary "competitive cycles" (e.g., Cycle A, Cycle B) based on title race intensity; these labels never made it into the League table and should not be inferred from the season text column.

Club operations staff sometimes classify stadiums as "TV-ready" versus "training-grade" based on broadcast cabling, camera gantries, and media workspaces, but these distinctions are only tracked in facilities management sheets and not directly encoded in the Match table.

Occasionally, match reports contain an informal note that a ground is a "backup venue" or "neutral venue" for a given competition year, but these statuses are not standardized codes and they do not systematically map to any specific value in the venue_ops_tier field.

Some tactical reports use the phrase "discipline without the ball" to describe teams that maintain shape in defensive phases; the authors often eyeball defensive pressure, aggression, and team width ratings, but they rarely document the exact numeric filters applied.

When data engineers validate the Match feed, they sometimes reference an internal "venue eligibility" checklist (minimum capacity, pitch dimensions, safety checks), but this checklist is not derived from nor does it directly alter the venue_ops_tier codes stored in the database.

Informal metrics like "streakiness in front of goal" are sometimes mentioned in commentary, typically meaning a player alternates hot scoring runs with long dry spells; people infer this from rolling goal totals over several matches rather than any dedicated field in the Player_Attributes table.

For some internal fan-facing products, marketing teams create a "clinical rating" out of 5 stars for forwards, but they usually derive this from a custom combination of finishing, shot power, and an editorial review, and it is not present as a numeric measure in this database.

The database contains multiple defensive-related ratings (pressure, aggression, team width, defender line), and different departments sometimes propose their own formulas for a "discipline index" or "structure score"; none of these experimental indices were ever standardized into an official column.

Historically, some users assumed that a low number of yellow and red cards defined a "disciplined" team, but card counts are influenced by refereeing style and match context, so this interpretation was discouraged for tactical classification and never encoded as a rule in the Team_Attributes schema.

Venue planners sometimes speak about a "hybrid operations tier" for grounds that share infrastructure across multiple clubs or competitions, but this is a planning convenience only and does not introduce any additional codes beyond what already exists in the venue_ops_tier column.

Some legacy documentation mentions a notional "Tier 0" for venues that had not yet passed initial inspections, but those locations were excluded from fixture scheduling altogether and never appear in the Match table or venue_ops_tier codes.

When discussing clubs "built for Europe", some authors take into account squad depth, travel logistics, and style suitability for continental refereeing, but this is a strategic notion and not a database attribute—no column marks a team as explicitly prepared for Champions League competition.

The analytics group experimented with a "big game index" intended to flag whether a team regularly appears in continental competitions, but this metric depended on external tournament data and was never integrated into the core Team_Attributes or Match schemas.

Some internal memos refer to the "UEFA cluster" of clubs within a league, roughly meaning those that frequently qualify for European tournaments; this is a narrative description only and is not directly derivable from the tables in this database without joining to separate competition records.

Editorial previews sometimes label a match as having a "European feel" when both clubs have recent continental experience, but this phrasing never maps onto any specific competition or season identifiers in the Match data itself.

The League table stores seasons in a text format like '2011/2012', but some business reports shorten this to a single label such as '2011-12 campaign' or group it under broader eras like 'post-2010', which are editorial conventions and not separate fields in the database.
